verbatim_text: It decodes a bunch of traces for each problem. Each trace has a terminal reward at the very end. You can average all of the terminal rewards for each particular problem and you get this average reward. You have this expectation, which is average reward for that problem. You can sum this up over all the problems in the training data and that gives you this total reward. So that is the objective, which is trying to look at this average terminal reward. How do we maximize it? Now, typically these rewards may be non-differentiable. And in many settings where we have these non-differentiable rewards, a typical framework that we use is reinforcement learning. In fact, DeepSeq R1 and a line of work on RL with verifiable rewards use the standard RL approach to optimize these terminal rewards and improve the performance of LLMs. But as it turns out, standard RL is not the best way to learn algorithms. And I'll give you an example of this in a didactic setting, which is actually a descriptor of a failure mode that appears very commonly in practice. And I'll show you the empirical results in a few slides for it. But the setting here is you want to train algorithms to prove math theorems.
